coredata.dc:title: N-doped graphene quantum dots and gold co-modified 3D printed electrode for sensitive detection of dopamine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Lian
Du, Xinhao
Jiang, Yanyu
Wen, Jie
Wang, Xuemei
Shuoti, Wuji
Peng, Ruihan
Liao, Meifang
Ou, Jing
Yang, Yaxin
Wang, Lujun
coredata.dc:description: 3D printing technology has unlimited potential in electrochemical sensing. In our work, a carbon black (CB)/polylactic acid (PLA) composite was employed as the raw material to fabricate 3D electrode (3DE) by 3D printing technique. Hydrothermally synthesized N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with gold particles were co-modified on the activated 3DE (A-3DE). The co-modification of NGQDs and gold particles increased the active sites on the surface of 3DE vegetated by carbon black nanonetworks and accelerated the electron transfer efficiency, creating a novel DA electrochemical sensing platform named as NGQDs@Au@3DE. Both microscopic and spectroscopic methods were employed to characterize the physicochemical properties of the synthesized NGQDs as well as the prepared NGQDs@Au@3DE. In addition, to study the ability of the NGQDs@Au@3DE to detect DA, a series of electrochemical methods were applied. The concentration range for detecting DA was between 0.01 nM and 30 nM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0094 nM. The NGQDs@Au@3DE showed excellent selectivity independent from the interfering substance of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA). In addition, the prepared DA electrochemical sensor showed excellent stability and potential for detecting real sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D printing technology
N-doped graphene quantum dots
Gold particles
Electrochemical sensor
Dopamine

Chemical and morphological characterisation of NGQDs
Optimization of preparation conditions for NGQDs@Au@3DE
The NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor for the detection of DA
The detection mechanism of DA on NGQDs@Au@3DE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs. (B) FT-IR spectrum of NGQDs. (C) TEM image of NGQDs (the inner graph shows the size distribution histogram of NGQDs). (D) High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of NGQDs.
SEM characterization of 3DEs: (A)(D) 3DE, (B)(E) A-3DE, (C)(F) Au@3DE; (G) HRTEM images of NGQDs@Au@3DE; (H) EDX characterization of NGQDs@Au@3DE.
(A) XPS analysis of 3DE, A-3DE, Au@3DE and NGQDs@Au@3DE. (B) C 1s, (C) O 1s, (D) N 1s and (E) Au 4f spectra of NGQDs@Au@3DE.
(A) CV and (B) EIS curves of 3DE, A-3DE, Au@3DE and NGQDs@Au@3DE with 10 mM of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− in 0.1 M KCl solution.

(A) The CV curves of DA at concentrations ranging from 0.01 nM to 30 nM were determined using NGQDs@Au@3DE in 0.2 M PBS; (B) The linear regression curve of DA concentration from 0.01 nM to 30 nM was detected on the NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor.
(A) The CV curves of 4 nM DA was determined using NGQDs@Au@3DE in 0.2 M PBS for 7 consecutive days; (B) Peak currents were measured in 4 nM DA solution three times a day for 7 consecutive days.
(A) The scheme for the preparation of NGQDs. (B) Preparation process of NGQDs@Au@3DE and redox reaction of DA.
Reaction mechanism of DA on NGQDs@Au@3DE.
The anti-interference ability of NGQDs@Au@3DE for the detection of 1 µM DA determination.
DA detection on NGQDs@Au@3DE compared to published sensors.
Application of NGQDs@Au@3DE for the detection of real samples.

head.ce:title: N-doped graphene quantum dots and gold co-modified 3D printed electrode for sensitive detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Medical Electrophysiology, Ministry of Education, Institute of Cardiovascular Research of Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Cardiovascular Research of Southwest

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Medical Electrophysiology, Ministry of Education, Institute of Cardiovascular Research of Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 3D printing technology has unlimited potential in electrochemical sensing. In our work, a carbon black (CB)/polylactic acid (PLA) composite was employed as the raw material to fabricate 3D electrode (3DE) by 3D printing technique. Hydrothermally synthesized N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with gold particles were co-modified on the activated 3DE (A-3DE). The co-modification of NGQDs and gold particles increased the active sites on the surface of 3DE vegetated by carbon black nanonetworks and accelerated the electron transfer efficiency, creating a novel DA electrochemical sensing platform named as NGQDs@Au@3DE. Both microscopic and spectroscopic methods were employed to characterize the physicochemical properties of the synthesized NGQDs as well as the prepared NGQDs@Au@3DE. In addition, to study the ability of the NGQDs@Au@3DE to detect DA, a series of electrochemical methods were applied. The concentration range for detecting DA was between 0.01 nM and 30 nM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0094 nM. The NGQDs@Au@3DE showed excellent selectivity independent from the interfering substance of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA). In addition, the prepared DA electrochemical sensor showed excellent stability and potential for detecting real sample.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a major neurotransmitter in the neurological system, with vital roles in the central nervous systems, acting as one of the most essential biomolecules in the body[1,2]. Aberrant DA levels are closely linked to the onset of diseases such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Huntington's[3,4]. Therefore, accurate measurement of DA levels is essential in the diagnosis and prevention of related diseases[5]. The accurate determination of DA with low concentration levels was considered as a very challenging task[6]. To solve the problem of accurate detection of the DA, numerous techniques have been developed, including high performance liquid chromatography[7], mass spectrometry[8] and fluorescence spectroscopy[9,10]. However, all these methods possess some disadvantages such as lengthy detection times, expensive, and difficult pre-processing[11]. Electrochemical approach has the benefits of easy material availability and straightforward operation compared to these classical methods[12]. In addition, electrochemistry is one of the most direct and promising methods owing to the inherent redox activity of DA[13], and thus has attracted a great deal of attentions in recent years[14].

3D printing also known as additive manufacturing (AM) is a digital manufacturing technique that offers new ways to produce complex geometries with high precision[15]. Recently, 3D printing technology has become a potent and economical instrument in the field of electrochemistry[16], enabling the large-scale production of devices such as batteries, wearable devices and electrodes (sensors)[17]. Compared to traditional electrochemical sensor manufacturing methods, 3D printing eliminates the complexity and monotony of conventional methods requiring multiple machining operations, reduces initial costs, and offers unique advantages in terms of the combination of materials used in production and product design[18,19]. The superior mechanical and electronic characteristics of the printed materials made using fused deposition modeling (FDM) make it a desirable option for 3D printing. 3D printed electrodes (3DEs) manufactured using FDM are typically made from conductive composites covered with non-conductive thermoplastics such as acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS) and polylactic acid (PLA)[20]. Therefore, pre-treatment approaches are employed to increase the conductivity of electrochemical sensors such as activation and modification of 3DEs surfaces[21]. The conductive material and active sites are exposed to improve the electrochemical activity of 3DEs by removing non-conductive material from the electrode surface[22–24]. Previous researches have shown that the electrochemical activity of 3DEs is enhanced by combining chemical and electrochemical treatments[25].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted attentions as new green nanomaterials due to their optical properties, surface functionalization, good solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility and good electrical conductivity[26]. In the field of electrochemical sensing, GQDs can be used as working electrodes or modified materials of sensors to achieve high sensitivity and selective detection of target molecules because of the high surface area, excellent electronic conductivity and chemical stability[27,28]. In addition, functionalization of GQDs with heteroatoms to modulate surface polarity, optical and electronic properties[29] can improve the performance of sensing[30], catalytic[31] and optoelectronic devices[32]. For example, GQDs doped with nitrogen atoms have attracted a lot of interest because they have more defect sites on their surface, which can facilitate interactions with target molecules[33]. Recently, it has been shown that the detection sensitivity can be improved through the synergistic interaction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with metals on electrode[34,35]. Among the metals, Au has particular catalytic properties. Due to the chemical inertness of the metal monomers, Au also has good biocompatibility, providing a suitable microenvironment for the immobilisation of biomolecules, making it as a good and ideal material for biosensing[36,37].

Herein, we report a novel 3D-printed electrochemical sensor for the detection of DA. Firstly, FDM 3D printing technology was used to create electrodes using carbon black (CB)/PLA as basic materials, and the synthesized N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) and gold particles were deposited on the 3DE to obtain NGQDs@Au@3DE and used for the specific detection of DA. The physical properties of NGQDs@Au@3DE and its ability to detect DA by electrochemical approach were studied, showing that the sensor displays excellent performance in sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and low limit of detection (LOD) compared to the published DA electrochemical sensor. This is the first time to modify the composite material of NGQDs and Au particles on 3DE to construct a novel 3D sensing platform for DA detection. As a result, the combination of NGQDs and Au can enhance the electrochemical performance of electrodes. Results also prove the potential of 3D printing technology using in electrochemical sensing.

The commercial CB/PLA filaments for preparing 3DEs were from Protopasta. Sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O) were obtained from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were acquired from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]·3H2O), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), anhydrous sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and disodium hydrogen phosphate anhydrous (Na2HPO4) were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4) and acetone were obtained from General Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Graphene oxide (GO) dispersion was attained from Maida Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). DA was bought from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals are analytical grade without further purification.

The 3DEs were produced on a Prusa i3 MK3 3D printer (Prusa Research, Czech Republic). The electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI660e electrochemical workstation (ChenHua, China). The spectral and energy spectral characteristics were analyzed using UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), Affinities-1S FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Electron, USA). Morphological analysis was performed using a SU8010 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan) and a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA).

NGQDs have been prepared hydrothermally using H2O2 as an etching agent, NH3·H2O as an auxiliary agent and the GO as a carbon source[38] (shown in Scheme 1 A). Firstly, 3 mL of NH3·H2O and 6 mL of H2O2 was added to a solution of 40 mL of 2 mg/mL GO aqueous dispersion, which was then mixed well and the solution was then diluted to 160 mL and sonicated for a specified period. The solution was then transferred to polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and reacted at 200°C for 10 h. Following the completion of the reaction and its cooling to room temperature, and then the solution was centrifuged at 6600 rpm for three minutes to obtain a yellowish supernatant. Subsequently, the unreacted NH3·H2O and H2O2 were removed by spin distillation, and finally the yellow powder was collected by freeze-drying. The powder was then solubilized in ultrapure water to prepare a dispersion of 2 mg/mL of NGQDs.

The 3DE model with dimensions of 1 mm in thickness and 6 mm in diameter was created using the Autodesk Fusion 360 platform. The designed model was converted into a solid 3DE using CB/PLA filament combined with advanced FDM 3D printing technology with a 60 °C bed temperature and a 215 °C nozzle temperature. The 3DE has been subject to a special treatment for the improvement of the electrochemical functionality of the electrodes. Initially, 3DE was soaked in DMF solution for 1 h to destroy the PLA structure. The electrodes were then rinsed with acetone and ethanol to effectively remove surplus PLA from the surface and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven for 1 h. Electrochemical experiments were performed using a three-electrode system with 3DE as the working electrode (WE), platinum wire as the counter electrode (CE) and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode (RE). The dried 3DE was immersed in 10 mL of 0.2 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 7.2 and then performed by applying a 2.5 V constant voltage for 300 s through the i-t method to obtain the activated 3D printed electrode (A-3DE).

A-3DE was dipped into a mixed solution of 50 mM HAuCl4 and 0.01 M HCl, and Au particles were electrodeposited on A-3DE by the i-t method with a constant potential of −0.9 V for 240 s. After repeating three times, and then the gold electrodeposited 3DE was washed with ultrapure water and dried to obtain Au@3DE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were electrodeposited utilizing the cyclic voltammetry (CV) method to prepare the NGQDs@Au@3DE. The electrode preparation process was shown in Scheme 1B. The electrodes were placed in the dispersion of 2 mg/mL of NGQDs performing 30 CV cycles at a scanning rate of 100 mV/s in the range of −1.5 V to 2.0 V.

The CV method was applied to study the electrochemical activity of 3DEs in the environment of [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- as redox probe with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and a test voltage range from −0.6 V to 1.2 V. Under the same experimental conditions, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test was performed with the electrodes at a constant potential of 0.24 V, with the aim of exploring the conductive nature of the electrodes and analyzing the impedance of different electrodes. The concentration of DA ranging from 0.01 to 30 nM in PBS solution was determined by the CV method. Three parallel runs of the EIS and CV experiments were conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and morphological characterisation of NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NGQDs were analyzed and characterized using both microscopic and spectroscopic methods. Fig. 1 A presents the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NGQDs in water. The absorption spectrum presents a characteristic absorption band at 208 nm and a weak shoulder peak at 300 nm. The absorption band at 208 nm is caused by electron transitions (π–π*) between C=C and C=N, and the absorption band at 300 nm belongs to the n-π* transition band of C=O.

FT-IR measurement of NGQDs was conducted at room temperature and the resulting spectra are shown in Fig. 1B. The spectrum shows a strong and broad band at 3190 cm−1, this is a result of the stretching vibration of the O–H bond. Furthermore, the bands at 1606 cm−1 and 1101 cm−1 belong to C=O and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1394 cm−1 could be attributed to the C–H bending vibration or the C–N stretching vibration.

TEM image was employed to analyze the morphology of the synthesized NGQDs. Fig. 1C shows that the synthesized NGQDs were spherical in shape and uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution. The ImageJ software was used to construct the distribution of NGQDs and the histogram of particle size exhibits a Gaussian distribution (n = 26) with a mean diameter of 2.91 ± 0.61 nm. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) results of NGQD are shown in Fig. 1D, which shows the lattice fringes of the sample, indicating that the synthesized NGQD has high crystallinity. The fringe spacing was measured to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite[39].

A series of characterization experiments were performed to analyze the chemical constituents and surface morphology of the different prepared electrodes. The SEM and TEM were applied to examine the surface morphology of the electrodes. It was observed that the untreated electrode was covered the PLA on the surface of the carbon black nanolayer, as seen in Fig. 2 A and Fig. 2D. Following the DMF treatment, as illustrated in Fig. 2B and Fig. 2E, the PLA on the surface of the electrode has been largely eliminated, forming a large number of crevices and exposing conductive carbon black nanolayer, which increases the redox reaction sites of the 3DE. In Fig. 2C and Fig. 2F, it can be observed that the electrode surface was uniformly covered by many gold particles with the average size of 12.92 ± 1.23 μm. This proves that the gold particles were effectively deposited on the electrode surface to obtain Au@3DE. Fig. 2G shows the HRTEM diagram of NGQDs@Au@3DE, and it can be seen that NGQDs is deposited on the surface of Au@3DE, indicating the successful preparation of NGQDs@Au@3DE. Combined with EDX analysis, the chemical composition distribution on the surface of the NGQDs@Au@3DE was examined. As displayed in Fig. 2H, the elements of C, N and Au were uniformly distributed on the electrode surface.

XPS was applied to characterize and analyze the elemental constitution and surface elemental bonding configurations of four 3DEs (Fig. 3 A). All four electrodes contain elements of C and O with binding energies of 285.4 eV and 532.4 eV, respectively. Au@3DE shows the peak Au 4f at the binding energy of 84.6 eV, and NGQDs@Au@3DE shows the peak N 1s at 399.0 eV. In Fig. 3B, the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of NGQDs@Au@3DE is divided into three peaks attributed to C=C (284.8 eV), C-N (285.6 eV) and C=O (287.2 eV), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3C, the O 1s spectra are concentrated at 531.7 and 533.5 eV, this shows that there are a lot of oxygenated functional groups[40]. The binding energies of the three peaks in the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s (Fig. 3D) are 399.6, 401.0, and 402.4 eV, namely pyridine N, pyrrolic N and graphite N, respectively[41]. The Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2 components are visible in the high resolution spectra of Au 4f (Fig. 3E). These are separated by 3.67 eV due to spin–orbit coupling[42]. Au@3DE shows a significant enhancement of the C 1s peak and a significant attenuation of the O 1s peak compared to 3DE, indicating that a large amount of PLA with high oxygen-containing elements was removed and the carbon fraction was exposed after DMF treatment. In Au@3DE, due to the coating of gold particles, the C 1s and O 1s peaks are weakened. The C 1s and O 1s peaks of NGQDs@Au@3DE are enhanced, the Au 4f peaks are weakened and the N 1s peaks were appeared at the same time compared to Au@3DE. These strongly confirm the successful deposition of NGQDs and Au particles on the A-3DE surface. Table S1 displays the proportions of four atoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of preparation conditions for NGQDs@Au@3DE
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs@Au@3DE were prepared by two methods. The first is the drop casting process, 2 mg/mL of NGQDs solution was dropped onto the surface of Au@3DE and dried overnight at room temperature to obtain NGQDs@Au@3DE. The second method to prepare NGQDs@Au@3DE was CV by electrodepositing NGQDs on gold particle modified 3DE. As shown in Fig. S1, the electrochemical activity of the NGQDs@Au@3DE prepared by the two methods was compared by the CV method (the test conditions were given in part 2.6). The electrochemical activity of NGQDs@Au@3DE prepared by electrodeposition is significantly better than that of NGQDs@Au@3DE prepared by drop casting. Therefore, NGQDs@Au@3DE prepared by electrodeposition is finally used for follow experiments.

The CV method (the test conditions were given in part 2.6) was used to study the effect of the number of electrodeposition cycles on the electrochemical properties of NGQDs@Au@3DE. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the effects of varying deposition cycles to the electrochemical activity of NGQDs@Au@3DE were demonstrated. The results indicated that the peak anode current (Ipa) and the peak cathode current (Ipc) of NGQDs@Au@3DE attained its maximum value when the number of cycles is 30. Therefore, 30 electrodeposition cycles were finally selected as the best experimental conditions for the preparation of NGQDs@Au@3DE.

The CV and EIS techniques were applied to further research the electrochemical characteristics of 3DEs. In Fig. 4 A, the CV curve of the non-activated 3DE is extremely flat, and it was difficult to observe its oxidation and reduction peaks, indicating that virtually little redox reaction occurred on the non-activated 3DE. After removing the surface PLA polymer, the current response value of A-3DE increase significantly. The excellent conductivity of gold particles increases the electron transfer capability of the electrode. Electrodeposition of NGQDs further improves the conductivity of the electrode. The results show that the electron transport between the electrode surface and the redox probe can be accelerated by the combined action of NGQDs and gold particles.

The EIS data were fitted using Zview software (Fig. 4B), which shows that the charge transfer resistance (Rct) gradually decreases with the gradual modification on the surface of 3DE with the values of Rct fitted to 1036.6 Ω (RSD = 0.71 %), 325.37 Ω (RSD = 1.74 %), 206.05 Ω (RSD = 1.91 %), 183.47 Ω (RSD = 5.58 %) for 3DE, A-3DE, Au@3DE and NGQDs@Au@3DE, respectively. The findings demonstrate that the electrochemical activation and co-modification of NGQDs and Au particles results in the appearance of many defect sites and the enhanced conductivity of 3DE, which increases the contact area with the supporting electrolyte and greatly reduces the electron transfer resistance. The results of EIS were in consistent with the data of CV. The combination of the two methods proves that the electrochemical properties of NGQDs@Au@3DE are significantly improved.

The classical Randles-Sevcik hypothesis was applied to study the surface activity of different 3DEs. All experiments were conducted in an environment with 0.1 M KCl solution ([Fe(CN)6]4-/3- as a sensitive electrochemical probe). Using the CV method to record the response curves of A-3DE, Au@3DE and NGQDs@Au@3DE at different scanning speeds, respectively. To compare the effect of different treatments to the active surface area of the electrodes, the linear relationship between peak redox current and scanning rate was examined. The Randles-Sevcik formula was applied to derive the specific active surface area of each electrode. The Randles-Sevcik equation is formulated as follows:
I p
= 269000
n
3 / 2
A C
D
1 / 2
v
1 / 2
where “ A ” is the active surface area of the electrode, “ n ” is the number of electrons transferred at the electrode during the redox process, “ D ” is the diffusion coefficient of K4[Fe(CN)6] (7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), “ C ” is the concentration of the redox probe in the solution, and “ v ” is the scanning rate.

As can be seen in Figs. S3A, B and C, the electrodes at each stage were scanned at scan rates between 5 and 150 mV s−1. As seen in Figs. S3D, E and F, the anodic currents and the square root of the scan rates of A-3DE, Au@3DE, and NGQDs@Au@3DE have a linear connection. The corresponding active areas are 0.209 cm2, 0.287 cm2 and 0.325 cm2 for A-3DE, Au@3DE and NGQDs@Au@3DE, respectively. This further fully proves that the electrochemical activity of 3DE could be gradually increased by co-modifying gold particles and NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor for the detection of DA
ce:section.ce:para: The CV approach was used to compare the detection capabilities of Au@3DE and NGQDs@Au@3DE for DA. The response of NGQDs@Au@3DE to 4 nM DA was significantly higher than that of Au@3DE (Fig. S4). The chemical structure of NGQDs makes it a good electric acceptor and can interact with the catechol structure of DA to promote the redox reaction of DA on the electrode. This proves that NGQDs@Au@3DE have excellent sensitivity for DA detection.

The internal and external environment for DA detection is complex, and components of biological urine and blood samples including uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA) can interfere the accurate detection of DA. To determine whether the NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor designed in the experiment is capable of resisting such interferences, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was applied to study the detection of DA by NGQDs@Au@3DE in PBS solutions containing DA, UA and AA (Fig. S5). Table 1 displays the influence of interfering substances on the DA peak current signal. Fig. S5A shows that AA has no significant oxidation peak in this assay system and has almost no effect on the detection of DA. In Fig. S5B, the oxidation peak potential of UA is different from that of DA. As shown in Fig. S5C, AA, DA and UA can be well distinguished by the difference in oxidation peak potentials and current response. The above experiments indicate that NGQDs@Au@3DE can detect low concentration of DA with good anti-interference ability.

In addition, to evaluate the prepared NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor's suitability for DA detection, the CV method was used to detect DA in a specific gradient concentration range. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the oxidation potential of DA is 0.209 V. The peak current increases steadily with gradually increasing DA concentration. By linearly fitting each concentration and corresponding peak current, the regression curve was obtained in the concentration range of 0.01 nM ∼ 30 nM. The linear regression equation is y = 1032.948x + 13730.605 (R2 = 0.9933) as shown in Fig. 5B. The formula 3δ/S was used to calculate the LOD value of DA detected by NGQDs@Au@3DE, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank response, S is the slope value of the regression curve, and the LOD is 0.0094 nM. As shown in Table 2 , compared to carbon-based sensors that detect DA utilizing different C-frames or organic compounds, the modified 3DE based on NGQDs and gold particles prepared has better detection performance and lower detection limits. This is due to the excellent electrochemical activity and electrical conductivity of gold particles and NGQDs, and their modification increases the surface area of the 3DE and obtains more reaction sites. DA is positively charged in a buffer solution with PH = 7.2, it can be concluded that the excellent LOD for the detection of DA by NGQDs@Au@3DE can be attributed to the π-π bond and strong electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged carboxylic acid group on NGQDs and the positively charged amine functional group on DA. Gold particles and NGQDs cooperate to accelerate electron transfer on the electrode surface to achieve high sensitivity detection. The detection mechanism of DA on NGQDs@Au@3DE surface is shown Scheme 2 .

To investigate the stability of the detection of DA by the prepared sensor, the DA response value at 4 nM in PBS solution was measured for one week using the same sensor with results displayed in Fig. 6 . The daily current response value of 4 nM DA on the sensor of NGQDs@Au@3DE does not differ significantly with RSD value of 6.27 %. According to the results, the prepared NGQDs@Au@3DE exhibits excellent stability for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanism of DA on NGQDs@Au@3DE
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetics of the electrochemical reaction of DA on NGQDs@Au@3DE were examined using the CV method. As shown in Fig. S6A, in the range of 20 to 200 mV s−1, the oxidation peak current of DA increases with the improvement of the scanning rate, while the peak potential of the reduction peak shifted to the left with increasing current. As seen in Fig. S6B and Fig. S6C, the peak current is linear to both the scan rate and the (scan rate)1/2. Therefore, to further research the electrochemical reaction mechanism of DA on NGQDs@Au@3DE, the relationship between log v and log Ip was discussed. The regression equation (y = 0.8341x-0.0665 (R2 = 0.9996)) confirms that a strong linear correlation also exists between log v and log Ip, as shown in Fig. S6D. The linear coefficients of log v and log Ip are close to 1, which proves that the redox process of DA is mainly dominated by the adsorption control process[50]. This electrochemical reaction of DA converted to dopamine quinone losses two protons and two electrons.

To evaluate the applicability of the prepared NGQDs@Au@3DE for actual sample detection, the commercially available dopamine hydrochloride injection as tested real samples was dissolved in PBS to concentrations of 6, 13 and 17 nM. The test values are presented in Table 3 and the calculated recoveries with standard addition are 98.83 % ∼ 99.70 % with RSD ranging from 0.67 % to 4.21 %. The capability of the NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor to detect DA in real samples is demonstrated by its excellent accuracy and dependability.

In summary, by combining 3D printing and electrochemical technologies, this research has developed a novel DA electrochemical sensing platform. For the first time, NGQDs and Au particles were modified together on a 3DE surface to prepare the NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor with the enhanced electrochemical activity because of the synergistic effect of NGQDs and Au particles. As a result of the enhanced interplay between the electrode surface and the target substances, the NGQDs@Au@3DE shows higher sensitivity, lower detection limits and superior electrochemical properties for DA detection compared to reported electrochemical sensors. The excellent stability and anti-interference ability of NGQDs@Au@3DE sensor is expected to expand the use of 3D printing technology and NGQDs in electrochemical sensing. In the future, the practical application and the long-term effectiveness of the 3D-printed electrochemical sensors can be further researched and explored.

Lian Zhong: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xinhao Du: Investigation, Data curation. Yanyu Jiang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Jie Wen: Methodology, Data curation. Xuemei Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Wuji Shuoti: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Ruihan Peng: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Meifang Liao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jing Ou: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yaxin Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Lujun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding of this work was supported by the Project of Sichuan Department of Science and Technology (2022NSFSC0622) and the Science and Technology Project of Luzhou (2021-JYJ-74).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113432.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
